title: Systematic frequency-collision analysis of the cross-resonance gate outside the straddling regime
authors: Shinichi Inoue, Shotaro Shirai, Shuhei Tamate, Shu Watanabe, Kohei Matsuura, Rui Li, Yasunobu Nakamura
abstract: Frequency crowding remains a major obstacle to scaling fixed-frequency transmon processors. Among the widely used all-microwave two-qubit gates, the cross-resonance (CR) gate is particularly sensitive to qubit-frequency spread because the conventional straddling regime condition constrains assignable qubit frequencies tightly and makes the system susceptible to frequency collisions. Here, we propose and analyze the CR gate outside the straddling regime, which we refer to as the far-detuned regime, and evaluate frequency collisions using a numerical method that remains accurate under high-intensity, smoothly ramped microwave drives. Based on this analysis, we perform systematic parameter sweeps and provide collision-free conditions that define designable frequency regions in which qubit frequencies can be assigned consistently with surrounding qubit frequencies. Furthermore, we formulate frequency allocation as a linear programming optimization on a unit-cell lattice with periodic boundary conditions to obtain an optimal allocation. We demonstrate that far-detuned designs significantly reduce collisions compared with designs in the straddling regime. Monte Carlo yield analysis indicates that 10% collision-free yield for a 1024-qubit square lattice at a 0.1% two-qubit-gate error threshold requires $σ_{\mathrm{f}}/2π\le 6.8~\mathrm{MHz}$. Our findings suggest that this is feasible with an approximately twofold reduction in the state-of-the-art qubit-frequency spread.
summary: The paper proposes moving the cross-resonance gate operation from the traditional straddling regime to a far-detuned regime to mitigate frequency collisions in transmon processors. By treating frequency allocation as a linear programming problem, the authors demonstrate that this approach allows for much tighter qubit-frequency spreads. This provides a scalable pathway for designing large-scale, fixed-frequency superconducting quantum processors.
main_problem: Frequency crowding in fixed-frequency transmon processors limits scalability because the conventional straddling regime for cross-resonance (CR) gates makes the system highly susceptible to frequency collisions.
main_result: The authors propose a 'far-detuned regime' for CR gates that significantly reduces frequency collisions and provide a linear programming optimization method for optimal frequency allocation in large lattices.
method: The study uses a numerical method accurate under high-intensity, smoothly ramped microwave drives, combined with systematic parameter sweeps and Monte Carlo yield analysis.
model_or_system: The system consists of fixed-frequency transmon qubits arranged in a square lattice, specifically analyzed up to a 1024-qubit scale.
key_observables: Two-qubit-gate error threshold and collision-free yield.
important_parameters: Qubit-frequency spread (sigma_f), frequency detuning (straddling vs. far-detuned regimes), and microwave drive intensity/ramping.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a unit-cell lattice with periodic boundary conditions for the optimization framework.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of frequency crowding, proposes the far-detuned CR gate regime, presents a numerical method for collision evaluation, details a linear programming approach for frequency allocation, and concludes with a Monte Carlo yield analysis for large-scale lattices.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in quantum computing and open quantum systems as it addresses a fundamental hardware scaling bottleneck (frequency crowding) using control-theory-inspired optimization and pulse-level analysis.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
